one_line_takeaway: A taxonomic survey (parameter optimization, network optimization, attack detection, protocol selection, key-metrics prediction) of how ML has been applied across ~40 prior QKD papers to improve secure key rate, QBER, and attack detection in CV-/DV-/MDI-/TF-QKD systems -- with no original experiments of its own and an explicit finding that the adversarial robustness of the ML detectors is itself an unaddressed gap in the field.
key_finding: The survey's own synthesis-level conclusions (stated in its Conclusion) are: (1) ML integration across the reviewed literature is associated with attack-detection accuracy up to ~98%, reduced QBER, and faster adaptation to environmental changes, though this is a rollup of disparate cited results, not a new measurement; (2) random forest emerges as the most consistently reliable model for protocol-selection tasks specifically, appearing as the top performer in both cited protocol-selection studies ([52], [53]); (3) real-time adaptability and cross-protocol universality remain open problems recurring across the parameter-optimization literature; (4) the security of the ML detectors themselves (adversarial robustness, poisoning) is a largely unaddressed gap in the surveyed literature -- an observation that is the survey's own analytical contribution.
core_contribution: This is a survey/taxonomy paper, not an experimental paper. Its own contribution is (1) a five-category taxonomy of how Machine Learning has been applied to Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) in the literature -- parameter optimization, network optimization, attack detection, protocol selection, and key-metrics prediction -- (2) three comparison tables (Table I comparing this survey's scope to four prior surveys [11]-[14]; Tables II-III tabulating ML model, performance, advantages, and limitations across ~15 protocol-specific and protocol-agnostic optimization papers) synthesizing and cross-comparing reported results from ~40 cited primary studies, and (3) a Challenges/Open-Problems section that is the survey authors' own analytical synthesis (not drawn from any single cited paper). The paper itself reports zero original experiments, datasets, or models -- every quantitative figure in the text and tables belongs to a cited work, not to this paper.
threat_model: Not the survey's own threat model (none is proposed), but the attacker classes discussed in the paper's Section VI (ML for QKD Attack Detection), drawn from cited work: photon-number-splitting (PNS) attacks, Trojan-horse attacks, local-oscillator (LO) intensity/calibration manipulation, detector saturation and wavelength attacks, detector blinding, impersonation/man-in-the-middle attacks (in IoT-deployed BB84, per [7]), denial-of-service via channel tampering/amplification (per [46]), and hybrid combinations thereof -- targeting the physical/hardware layer of CV-QKD and DV-QKD systems rather than the QKD math itself. The survey explicitly notes (Challenges section) that the security of the ML models used for this detection -- i.e., adversarial examples or poisoning attacks against the detectors themselves -- is "rarely addressed in the existing literature," which the survey flags as a gap rather than resolves. This paper is about ML applied to secure/optimize a cryptographic protocol (QKD); it is not about adversarial ML or securing AI systems.
limitations: From the paper's own Challenges/Open-Problems section (Section IX-A/B): (1) computational overhead of deep-learning models is often too resource-intensive for time-critical or low-power QKD deployment; (2) most surveyed ML approaches are trained on simulation-generated rather than real-world experimental data, raising generalizability concerns under real hardware noise/instability; (3) scalability is limited -- ML shows strong results on small-scale/single-link setups but is largely unvalidated on large, distributed multi-protocol quantum networks; (4) security of the ML models themselves (adversarial attacks, model poisoning) is rarely addressed in the surveyed literature; (5) lack of standardized evaluation metrics/benchmarks across studies makes cross-paper comparison unreliable -- an issue the survey itself partially inherits, since it aggregates heterogeneous, non-standardized reported numbers from different simulation setups into its comparison tables without normalizing methodology.

datasets_used: None used by the survey itself. Datasets referenced belong to the ~40 surveyed primary studies, and are described by the survey as predominantly simulation-generated (e.g., orbital-model simulations for satellite CV-QKD in [36], synthetic 8000-record simulation datasets in [29] and [52]) with comparatively few real experimental datasets; the survey itself (Challenges section) flags this simulation-dependence as a generalizability risk.
quantitative_results: [26]: multi-layer quantum+ML architecture -- DL model improves key generation rate by 25%; unsupervised anomaly detector increases eavesdropping detection rate by 7.8%; RL agent reduces QBER by 33.3%; overall system efficiency improvement of 8.2%
[27]: BPNN calibration for 3-intensity MDI-QKD -- ~0.186 s inference, ~100x speedup vs. local search algorithm (LSA), key rate ~85% of optimal
[21]: unified FCNN (2 hidden layers, ReLU) across BB84/MDI-QKD/TF-QKD -- 10^2-10^4x speedup, 1-3 ms inference, key rate 95-99.99% of optimal
[28]: double-scanning pipeline for 4-intensity MDI-QKD benchmarking RF/MLP/GLM/XGBoost/KNN/Decision Tree -- RF best accuracy with R^2 = 0.99, microsecond-scale inference
[29] Ding et al.: RF regression for 3-intensity MDI-QKD/BB84 -- >99% prediction accuracy, key rate 99.41% of optimal, 8000 parameter sets processed in 2 seconds
[30] Yi et al.: enhanced RF with OOB pruning for MDI-QKD -- R^2 = 0.995, MSE = 1.81x10^-4, no experimental comparison against other ML baselines
[31] Kang et al.: BPNN/RBFNN/GRNN for TF-QKD -- RBFNN key rate ~99.97% of LSA benchmark, BPNN inference 4.3-27 microseconds
[22] Dong et al.: XGBoost/BPNN/RF for symmetric TF-QKD -- key-rate ratio 99.63% vs. exhaustive search, MSE ~2.2x10^-5, 0.005 s for 3000 samples, 300,000x speedup over conventional search
[24]: SVR for CV-QKD LO/signal drift stabilization -- 1.2 ms/cycle, achieves theoretical optimal key rates without added hardware
[9]: DNN + polynomial fitting for BB84 polarization prediction -- SKR ~4.5 Mb/s, QBER reduced from 6.1% to >1.5%
[15] Liu et al.: LSTM for DV-QKD phase drift correction -- duty cycle improved from 50% to 83% without compromising QBER

[36]: ResFCN18 NN for satellite-to-ground CV-QKD modulation-variance (Vmod) prediction -- ~99.5% accuracy, 10^3-10^4x faster than conventional computation, SKR gain up to 22 dB, secure window extended +192 s at 710 km
[38]: error-prediction + stabilizer codes for post-processing -- QBER down 12%, SKR up 15%
[39] Liang et al.: NN+KNN classification for CV-QKD noise suppression -- excess noise down 72%, error variance down 26%, SKR up 18%
[40] Zhang et al.: LSTM autoencoder nonlinear compensation for CV-QKD -- 9.57 Mbps SKR at 500 MHz, 18 Mbps at 1 GHz, excess noise ~10^-3 SNU
[41] Nozari & Uysal: DNN for underwater BB84 SPAD detector-parameter optimization -- MSE ~10^-4, R^2 near 1, QBER suppressed by up to two orders of magnitude over 10-40 m underwater links
[42] Yi et al.: GA-Elman RNN for passive CV-MDI-QKD transmittance prediction -- average error reduced from 2.814% (standard Elman) to 0.506% (GA-Elman)
[43]: NN/KNN/XGBoost for QKD frequency assignment in multi-band elastic optical networks -- 99% accuracy, computation time reduced from 637 s to 0.09 s
[48] Ding et al. (MADS): DBSCAN + one-vs-rest SVM (Gaussian kernel) for 8-class CV-QKD attack classification -- accuracy/precision/recall/F1 ~0.9996

[49] Jin et al.: graph isomorphism network (GIN) for CV-QKD attack detection, three graph-construction methods -- Method I 97.8%, Method II 86.4% (100% attack-vs-no-attack detection efficiency), Method III 98.8% on in-distribution test; Methods I/III maintain >95% accuracy on distance/VA transfer sets; anomaly module on Method III flags partially unknown attacks at ~85% effectiveness
[51] Chou et al.: GMM + Bayesian classifier for QBER-based Trojan-horse detection -- >98% detection over fibers up to 30 km

[52] Nayana JS et al.: KNN/CNN/SVM/DT/RF for protocol selection (BB84/MDI-QKD/TF-QKD) -- RF best at ~98.2% accuracy, computation time reduced from 8-10 hours to under 1 second, ~8000-record simulation dataset
[53] Ren et al.: RF/SVM/KNN/MNB/CNN for protocol selection -- RF best, prediction accuracy exceeding 98%
[55]: autoencoder NN predicting QBER and final key length (FKL) -- >99% accuracy
[54]: ANN predicting SKR for homodyne-detection discrete-modulated CV-QKD -- 99.2% reliability, 6-8 orders of magnitude speedup over traditional numerical methods
[56]: Bayesian-optimized NN for QPSK CV-QKD SKR prediction -- 99.15% (heterodyne), 99.59% (homodyne), ~10^7x speedup over traditional numerics
[58]: RF regressor predicting Strehl ratio for free-space channel turbulence -- MAPE 4.44%, improved to 3.86% with fidelity as an added feature
Survey-level rollup (Conclusion, not a new measurement): ML integration across surveyed literature associated with attack-detection accuracy up to ~98%
baselines_compared: Not the survey's own baselines -- these are baselines/reference methods that the surveyed primary studies compared their ML models against: local search algorithm (LSA) / exhaustive search for QKD parameter optimization (in [27],[21],[29],[31],[22]); conventional convex-optimization/iterative numerical methods for SKR/QBER computation (in [54],[56]); Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) vs. Unscented Kalman Filter for CV-QKD phase tracking (in [32]); standard/vanilla RF vs. enhanced RF with OOB pruning (in [30]); standard Elman RNN vs. GA-Elman (in [42]); hop-count shortest-path routing vs. LSTM-based traffic prediction (in [44]); among ML models compared to each other within single studies: RF vs. SVM vs. KNN vs. MNB vs. CNN for protocol selection (in [53]) and KNN vs. CNN vs. SVM vs. DT vs. RF (in [52]).
